findings.title: Container runs as root — no USER directive in Dockerfile
findings.description: The Dockerfile does not set a non-root USER. The container process will run as UID 0 (root), violating principle of least privilege. If the application is compromised, the attacker gains root-level access within the container.
findings.remediation: Add USER node (or a dedicated non-root UID) before the CMD instruction.

findings.title: Base image uses mutable tag — no digest pinning
findings.description: FROM node:20-alpine uses a mutable tag. Tag can be silently updated to a malicious or vulnerable image.
findings.remediation: Pin base image to immutable digest: FROM node:20-alpine@sha256:<digest>

findings.title: npm install without --ignore-scripts allows malicious lifecycle hooks
findings.description: RUN npm install --production does not use --ignore-scripts. A compromised dependency could execute arbitrary code during install via postinstall hooks.
findings.remediation: Use RUN npm ci --omit=dev --ignore-scripts for production Docker builds.

findings.title: No package lockfile — dependency versions not pinned
findings.description: No package-lock.json or yarn.lock present. npm install resolves to potentially different versions each build, enabling supply chain attacks via dependency confusion or version drift.
findings.remediation: Commit package-lock.json and use npm ci instead of npm install in CI/CD.

findings.title: lodash@4.17.10 — prototype pollution (CVE-2019-10744, CVE-2020-8203)
findings.description: lodash 4.17.10 is vulnerable to prototype pollution via _.merge(), _.defaultsDeep(), and _.zipObjectDeep(). An attacker controlling merge input can pollute Object.prototype and potentially achieve RCE or privilege escalation.
